evidence: We apply data, automation, and AI to improve the speed, accuracy, and usability of our products.
we expect an increased reliance on automation, proactive intelligence capabilities and AI tools that we believe will enhance productivity and maintain operational efficiency
We plan to continue to operate at this smaller size and are continuing to look at ways to improve our efficiency through a combination of AI automation, prioritization of our scope
Square s AI-enabled tools are designed to reduce manual work, support sellers' decision-making, and enable sellers to manage their operations and finances more effectively
we have incorporated and expect to continue to incorporate AI technologies, including generative AI and AI agents, into our products and technologies
we have made other technology we developed, such as AI agents, available under such licenses
Our use of AI depends on third-party large language models that we do not control, and these models may generate inaccurate, biased, or unexpected outputs that conflict with our business objectives

AI-enabled features in our products may generate outputs that could be interpreted as regulated advice, even if unintended or automated... we could face reputational harm, regulatory scrutiny, enforcement actions or liability claims

any reassignment of responsibilities, together with potential increased reliance on automation and AI tools to support certain legal, regulatory compliance and risk management functions, may limit our capacity and introduce additional oversight risks

limitations: No quantified AI-specific capex or budget figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 based on named initiatives and named infrastructure references without dollar amounts or headcount figures.

Narrative concreteness scored 4 based on named deployed use cases (AI agents, AI-enabled seller tools, generative AI in products) with operational detail, but no quantified business results (e.g., % efficiency gains, volume handled) were disclosed in the excerpts.

AI risk disclosure is extensive and company-specific with named mechanisms (third-party LLM dependence, regulated-advice liability, compliance/governance risks from workforce reduction), supporting score 4; no formal quantified risk metrics or named oversight governance bodies were cited, which would be required for score 5.
